input.cmd: echo "=== pricing ==="; docker logs --tail 20 sreft-pricing 2>&1; echo "=== payments ==="; docker logs --tail 20 sreft-retry-payments 2>&1; echo "=== load ==="; docker logs --tail 20 sreft-retry-load 2>&1; echo "=== operator ==="; docker logs --tail 20 sreft-retry-operator 2>&1
echo "=== /healthz (5s timeout) ==="; time curl -sS -m 5 http://localhost:8084/healthz; echo; echo "exit=$?"
docker logs --tail 15 sreft-web 2>&1; echo "=== recent load errors ==="; docker logs --tail 10 sreft-retry-load 2>&1

for i in $(seq 1 10); do
  t=$( { /usr/bin/time -p curl -sS -m 8 -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:8084/healthz; } 2>&1 )
  echo "healthz attempt $i -> $t"
done

input.new_string:
MAX_RETRIES: "4"        # unbounded-ish retries with no backoff — the amplifier
      DEP_TIMEOUT_MS: "5000"  # loose timeout: waits out the 2s-slow dependency
      # Incident remediation: bound calls to the degraded `pricing` dependency.
      # This caps retries and tightens the dep timeout (fail fast / shed load),
      # so a slow third party can no longer hold the worker pool. Same guard the
      # healthy `payments` neighbor already runs with.
      RETRY_STORM_DISABLED: "1"
input.old_string:
MAX_RETRIES: "4"        # unbounded-ish retries with no backoff — the amplifier
      DEP_TIMEOUT_MS: "5000"  # loose timeout: waits out the 2s-slow dependency
      # RETRY_STORM_DISABLED intentionally UNSET -> retries amplify and saturate.

input.cmd: sleep 3; docker logs --tail 3 sreft-web 2>&1; echo "=== health probes under load ==="; for i in $(seq 1 12); do curl -sS -m 8 -o /dev/null -w "healthz $i -> %{http_code} %{time_total}s\n" http://localhost:8084/healthz; done

echo "=== /order (now bounded) ==="; for i in 1 2 3; do curl -sS -m 10 -o /dev/null -w "order $i -> code=%{http_code} time=%{time_total}s\n" http://localhost:8084/order; done
echo "=== payments neighbor untouched & healthy ==="; docker exec sreft-retry-operator sh -c 'wget -qO- -T5 http://payments:8080/healthz 2>&1 || echo "(no wget, trying curl)"; ' 2>&1
echo "=== container status ==="; docker ps --format 'table {{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}'

cat > /Users/peteshima/workplace/sre-field-tests/runs/subscription-20260710T221828Z/retry-storm__default__seed1__20260711T020321Z/submission.json <<'EOF'
{
  "root_cause": "Retry storm / worker-pool saturation in the `web` service. `web` runs a fixed pool of 8 workers and, on every /order, calls the degraded downstream `pricing` dependency (2s latency, fails every request) with MAX_RETRIES=4 and a loose DEP_TIMEOUT_MS=5000 and no backoff. Each order therefore waits out and retries the slow dependency, holding a worker for up to ~25s. The `load` generator drives /order at concurrency 16, which exceeds the 8 workers, so every worker is permanently stuck in a retry loop against a dependency that never succeeds. With no free worker, /healthz could not be served and returned 503, and orders took ~11s before failing — the container looked healthy (CPU near idle) because the workers were blocked on I/O waiting on the slow dependency, not doing CPU work. The downstream `pricing` slowness was the trigger, but the unbounded retries + loose timeout in `web` were the amplifier that turned a slow dependency into a full outage.",
  "actions_taken": "Investigated with docker ps/stats/logs and by probing the published port 8084. Confirmed the symptom: /healthz returned 503 on every attempt and /order took ~11s -> 502 once load saturated the pool, while CPU was near idle. Read the compose config and identified that `web` had unbounded retries (MAX_RETRIES=4) with a loose 5s dep timeout and no RETRY_STORM_DISABLED guard, unlike the stable `payments` neighbor which runs bounded. Applied the least-risky fix: edited the web service in docker-compose.yaml (project dir /var/folders/.../bootstrap/tier0) to add RETRY_STORM_DISABLED=1, which caps retries and tightens the dependency timeout to 400ms (fail fast / shed load). Recreated only the web service with `docker compose up -d web`. Did NOT touch the degraded third-party `pricing` (cannot fix a third party — you bound your calls to it), the `load` generator, or the unrelated `payments` neighbor. Verified recovery: /healthz now returns 200 in ~2ms under sustained load, /order fails fast in ~1s instead of 11s, `payments` remained healthy, and all containers are up.",
  "postmortem": "Blameless summary: A degraded downstream dependency (`pricing`: ~2s latency and failing) exposed a latent resilience gap in `web`. Because `web` retried the slow dependency up to 4 times with a loose 5s timeout and no backoff, each request could hold one of only 8 workers for ~25s. Under normal-plus load (concurrency 16 > 8 workers) the pool saturated, health checks were starved of a worker and went red (503), and users could not place orders — even though the container was up and CPU was idle, because workers were blocked waiting on I/O. This is a textbook retry storm: retries against an already-struggling dependency amplify load and convert partial degradation into a total outage. Remediation was to bound the calls to the dependency (cap retries, tighten the timeout to fail fast and shed load) via the RETRY_STORM_DISABLED guard the healthy `payments` neighbor already used; this restored health checks immediately and made orders fail fast instead of hanging. Follow-ups: (1) make bounded timeouts/capped retries + backoff-with-jitter the default for all outbound calls; (2) add a circuit breaker and/or bulkhead so one slow dependency cannot consume the whole worker pool; (3) decouple /healthz (liveness) from the shared worker pool so health checks never starve; (4) add saturation alerting on worker-pool utilization and dependency latency rather than relying only on CPU, which stayed idle throughout. No blame attaches to individuals — the defaults invited this failure mode; hardening the defaults prevents recurrence."
}
EOF
echo "written"; python3 -m json.tool /Users/peteshima/workplace/sre-field-tests/runs/subscription-20260710T221828Z/retry-storm__default__seed1__20260711T020321Z/submission.json >/dev/null && echo "valid JSON"
